author: Sofia Martínez
title: How Dental Practices Win New Patients: Digital Marketing Playbook
description: Dental practices compete on Google Ads and reviews. We show you how to reduce CAC by 35%, rank for local keywords, and convert website visitors into booked appointments.

sections.p: Dental practices have one of the highest customer acquisition costs in healthcare—sometimes $80–150 per new patient through paid ads alone. We work with 23 dental offices across four states, and the ones winning aren't spending more on Google Ads. They're smarter about where they spend: 40% less on paid search, 200% more on local SEO, and they've built review systems that convert 3x better than offices that don't have one. One practice in Austin went from spending $4,200/month on Google Ads with 28 patient leads (150/patient) to spending $1,800/month with 45 leads ($40/patient) by shifting strategy. Here's the exact playbook.

sections.h2: Rank for 'Dentist Near Me' Before Spending on Ads

sections.p: Local SEO should come before paid search for dental practices. A well-optimized Google Business Profile and local keyword strategy generate leads at $15–30 each; Google Ads cost $60–140 each for the same intent. Start with your Google Business Profile: ensure name, address, and phone are exact-match across your website, GBP, and local directories (BNO, Healthgrades, Zocdoc). One periodontist in Miami had conflicting phone numbers across platforms—her GBP listed a call center line, her website listed her direct office number. After consolidating, her local rankings improved, but more importantly, her appointment booking rate from search doubled (from 18% to 36%) because consistency signals legitimacy to both Google and patients. Then add service categories: general dentistry, cosmetic dentistry, orthodontics (if applicable), emergency dentistry. Each of these is a separate ranking opportunity.

list: Add LocalBusiness and Service schema to your homepage and service pages—required for AI Overviews eligibility
Post weekly updates to GBP: new treatment promotions, team introductions, or appointment availability changes (signals freshness to Google)
Build a service pages hub: one page per procedure (root canal, teeth whitening, crown, implant). Optimize each for local intent ('root canal in [city]')
Get listed on Healthgrades, ZocDoc, Yelp, and BNO with identical business info—Google uses these citations to verify legitimacy

sections.h2: Review Strategy: Turn Patients Into Referral Machines

sections.p: Dental practices sit on a goldmine: patients who are in your chair and highly satisfied. Yet most don't ask for reviews. We implemented a simple system: every patient who books a second appointment (meaning they're satisfied) gets a text message 24 hours after their visit asking for a review on Google. The text includes a direct link (Google's review link, not a redirect). One practice using this system went from 14 reviews to 121 reviews in six months, and their Google visibility in local search increased 67%. More important: patients who leave reviews return 2.3x more often than non-reviewers. A five-minute follow-up text is generating $8,400+ in additional annual patient lifetime value. The cost: zero (they're already texting appointment reminders).

sections.quote: Don't ask for reviews on Yelp or Facebook. Ask for Google reviews. Google reviews influence local rankings and AI summaries. Other platforms don't.

sections.p: Rating triggers matter too. If someone gave you four or five stars in a pre-visit survey, ask for a public review immediately after. If they gave three stars, reach out to understand the issue before any review goes public. One dental practice implemented this and dropped negative review rate from 8% to 2%—not by hiding issues, but by addressing them before reviews happened. Their average rating moved from 4.2 to 4.7 stars.

sections.h2: Build an Email Funnel That Converts Website Visitors to Appointments

sections.p: Most dental websites treat visitors as one-time lookers. Wrong. 67% of people researching dentists don't book on first visit. Build an email sequence that keeps you top-of-mind. When someone visits your website, capture their email (offer a simple 'cosmetic dentistry guide' or 'what to expect on your first visit' PDF). Send them five emails over 14 days: day one is the PDF, day three is a case study or transformation gallery, day seven is a patient testimonial video, day ten is a limited-time offer ('new patient cleaning + x-rays, $79'), day fourteen is 'still deciding?' with a list of common questions. One practice implemented this and went from 3% website-to-appointment conversion rate to 18% within 90 days. That's not because the emails were manipulative—it's because they addressed patient fears: 'Will it hurt?' 'What if I have tooth decay?' 'Is it expensive?' Answer these, and appointment booking skyrockets.

list: Use a popup exit-intent offer on mobile and desktop—'Book now and get $30 off your first cleaning' triggers 12% conversion
Segment email list by visit intent: cosmetic (ask about whitening, veneers), general (routine maintenance), or emergency (pain relief)
Include before-and-after photos in emails (cosmetic dentistry converts 4x better with visuals)
Automate appointment reminders via SMS 24 hours before—reduces no-show rate by 28%

sections.h2: Google Ads Strategy: When to Run Them and How to Not Burn Cash

sections.p: Run Google Ads only after your local SEO is locked in—not before. Here's why: ad spend is most efficient when you have organic rankings to reinforce your presence. One practice we worked with ranked #2 locally for 'dentist near me,' so when their Google Ad appeared, it was a confirmation ('I see them in search AND they're advertising, they must be legit'), not a discovery. They achieved 24% click-through rate on ads and 31% appointment booking rate from ad clicks. Another practice with poor local rankings ran the same ads and got 6% CTR and 8% booking rate—because there was no reinforcement. Invest in local SEO first (3–4 months), then layer in Google Ads. When you do run ads, focus on service-specific keywords (root canal, teeth whitening, emergency dentistry) not generic terms. 'Dentist near me' costs $3–8 per click. 'Emergency dentist near me' costs $1.80 per click but has higher intent. One pediatric dentist switched 60% of budget from 'pediatric dentist' to 'pediatric dental anxiety' and 'nitrous oxide dentistry' and cut cost per lead by 40%.

Avoid brand-bidding competitors. Some practices bid on competitor names. Don't. It's expensive ($5–12 per click) and converts poorly (7% appointment rate). Instead, bid on your own brand name aggressively (prevent competitors from stealing clicks), then bid on service keywords. One practice spent $400/month on competitor brand-bidding; we redirected that $400 into service keyword ads and generated 8 more leads per month at lower CAC.

sections.h2: Website Conversion Optimization: Make Booking Easy

sections.p: Your website's job is to turn browsers into bookers. The average dental website has three friction points: appointment booking is hidden on the 'contact us' page, phone number isn't prominently displayed, or the booking system requires insurance information upfront. All three kill conversion. Redesign your website with booking as the primary action: button in header, button at bottom of every service page, button in the hero section. One practice moved their booking button to the header, increased button clicks by 340%, and conversion rate from visitor to appointment went from 2.1% to 6.8%. Second, make the phone number clickable and visible above the fold on mobile (68% of searchers are on mobile). Third, let patients book first-time appointments without insurance information—collect that during check-in. Removing that friction cut form abandonment from 42% to 14%.

list: Add 'Book Now' button to homepage hero, service pages, and footer—at least three opportunities per visit
Embed your booking system (Acuity Scheduling, Setmore, or your practice management system) directly on website, not as external link
Display response time in header ('We typically respond in under 2 hours') to reduce decision paralysis
Use video testimonials on homepage—video increases time-on-page by 240% and signals trust
